examples.title: Algorithm: Critical Section Problem Solution
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the critical section problem with mutex-based solution satisfying mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Critical Section with Mutex
INPUT: Shared variable counter, Mutex lock
OUTPUT: Correctly incremented counter

DATA STRUCTURES:
 - lock : mutex
 - counter : integer (shared resource)

// Thread/Process Structure
PROCEDURE Process_i
 WHILE true DO
 // Entry Section - Acquire lock
 lock.acquire()
 
 // Critical Section - Access shared resource
 temp = counter
 temp = temp + 1
 counter = temp
 
 // Exit Section - Release lock
 lock.release()
 
 // Remainder Section - Non-critical work
 do_other_work()
 END WHILE
END PROCEDURE

// Semaphore Implementation
PROCEDURE wait(S)
 S = S - 1
 IF S < 0 THEN
 block_current_thread()
 END IF
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE signal(S)
 S = S + 1
 IF S <= 0 THEN
 wakeup_one_thread()
 END IF
END PROCEDURE

// Producer-Consumer with Counting Semaphore
ALGORITHM Producer_Consumer
 INPUT: emptySlots (counting semaphore, initial=n), 
 fullSlots (counting semaphore, initial=0),
 mutex (binary semaphore, initial=1)
 
 PRODUCER:
 WHILE true DO
 item = produce_item()
 wait(emptySlots) // Decrement available slots
 wait(mutex) // Enter critical section
 add_to_buffer(item)
 signal(mutex) // Exit critical section
 signal(fullSlots) // Increment filled slots
 END WHILE
 
 CONSUMER:
 WHILE true DO
 wait(fullSlots) // Wait for items
 wait(mutex) // Enter critical section
 item = remove_from_buffer()
 signal(mutex) // Exit critical section
 signal(emptySlots) // Free up a slot
 consume_item(item)
 END WHILE
END ALGORITHM
examples.explanation: The pseudocode demonstrates three key concepts: (1) The critical section problem where multiple threads access shared 'counter' - the mutex ensures only one thread enters the critical section at a time. (2) The fundamental wait(S) and signal(S) semaphore operations that decrement/increment the semaphore value and handle blocking/waking. (3) The classic Producer-Consumer problem using counting semaphores to coordinate a bounded buffer - emptySlots tracks available space, fullSlots tracks filled slots, and mutex ensures buffer access is atomic.

examples.title: Python: Thread Synchronization with Mutex and Semaphore
examples.description: Python implementation demonstrating mutex locks, binary semaphores, and counting semaphores for coordinating threads
examples.code:
import threading
import time
import random
from collections import deque

# ============================================
# Example 1: Critical Section with Mutex
# ============================================
class Counter:
 def __init__(self):
 self.value = 0
 self.lock = threading.Lock() # Mutex for mutual exclusion
 
 def increment(self):
 """Thread-safe increment - demonstrates critical section"""
 # Entry section: acquire lock
 with self.lock:
 # Critical section: access shared resource
 temp = self.value
 time.sleep(0.001) # Simulate processing time
 temp = temp + 1
 self.value = temp
 # Exit section: lock automatically released
 
 def increment_atomic(self):
 """Atomic increment using lock"""
 with self.lock:
 self.value += 1

def worker(counter, iterations):
 """Worker function that increments counter multiple times"""
 for _ in range(iterations):
 counter.increment_atomic()

def demonstrate_mutex():
 """Demonstrate mutual exclusion with multiple threads"""
 counter = Counter()
 num_threads = 5
 iterations = 1000
 
 threads = []
 for i in range(num_threads):
 t = threading.Thread(target=worker, args=(counter, iterations))
 threads.append(t)
 
 # Start all threads
 start_time = time.time()
 for t in threads:
 t.start()
 
 # Wait for all to complete
 for t in threads:
 t.join()
 
 elapsed = time.time() - start_time
 expected = num_threads * iterations
 print(f"Mutex Example:")
 print(f" Expected: {expected}, Actual: {counter.value}")
 print(f" Time: {elapsed:.4f}s")
 print(f" Correct: {counter.value == expected}\n")

# ============================================
# Example 2: Binary Semaphore (Mutex) Implementation
# ============================================
class BinarySemaphore:
 """Binary semaphore implementation (0 or 1)"""
 def __init__(self, initial=1):
 self.value = initial
 self.lock = threading.Lock()
 self.condition = threading.Condition(self.lock)
 
 def wait(self):
 """P operation - decrement (acquire)"""
 with self.condition:
 while self.value == 0:
 self.condition.wait() # Block until signal
 self.value = 0
 
 def signal(self):
 """V operation - increment (release)"""
 with self.condition:
 self.value = 1
 self.condition.notify() # Wake up one waiting thread

def binary_semaphore_demo():
 """Demonstrate binary semaphore for mutual exclusion"""
 semaphore = BinarySemaphore(initial=1)
 shared_resource = []
 
 def access_resource(thread_id):
 semaphore.wait() # Acquire
 shared_resource.append(thread_id)
 print(f"Thread {thread_id} entered critical section")
 time.sleep(random.uniform(0.1, 0.3))
 print(f"Thread {thread_id} exiting critical section")
 semaphore.signal() # Release
 
 threads = []
 for i in range(3):
 t = threading.Thread(target=access_resource, args=(i,))
 threads.append(t)
 t.start()
 
 for t in threads:
 t.join()
 
 print(f"Binary Semaphore: All threads completed\n")

# ============================================
# Example 3: Counting Semaphore - Producer Consumer
# ============================================
class CountingSemaphore:
 """Counting semaphore implementation"""
 def __init__(self, initial=0):
 self.value = initial
 self.lock = threading.Lock()
 self.condition = threading.Condition(self.lock)
 
 def wait(self):
 """P operation - decrement and block if negative"""
 with self.condition:
 while self.value <= 0:
 self.condition.wait()
 self.value -= 1
 
 def signal(self):
 """V operation - increment and wake one thread"""
 with self.condition:
 self.value += 1
 self.condition.notify()

class BoundedBuffer:
 """Producer-Consumer problem with bounded buffer"""
 def __init__(self, capacity=5):
 self.buffer = deque(maxlen=capacity)
 self.capacity = capacity
 self.empty_slots = CountingSemaphore(capacity) # Available space
 self.full_slots = CountingSemaphore(0) # Items available
 self.mutex = threading.Lock() # Binary semaphore
 
 def produce(self, item):
 """Producer adds item to buffer"""
 self.empty_slots.wait() # Wait for empty slot
 self.mutex.wait() # Enter critical section
 self.buffer.append(item)
 print(f"Produced: {item}, Buffer: {list(self.buffer)}")
 self.mutex.signal() # Exit critical section
 self.full_slots.signal() # Signal item available
 
 def consume(self):
 """Consumer removes item from buffer"""
 self.full_slots.wait() # Wait for item
 self.mutex.wait() # Enter critical section
 item = self.buffer.popleft()
 print(f"Consumed: {item}, Buffer: {list(self.buffer)}")
 self.mutex.signal() # Exit critical section
 self.empty_slots.signal() # Signal slot available
 return item

def producer_consumer_demo():
 """Demonstrate producer-consumer with counting semaphores"""
 buffer = BoundedBuffer(capacity=3)
 produced_items = []
 consumed_items = []
 
 def producer(count):
 for i in range(count):
 item = f"Item-{i}"
 buffer.produce(item)
 produced_items.append(item)
 time.sleep(random.uniform(0.1, 0.3))
 
 def consumer(count):
 for _ in range(count):
 item = buffer.consume()
 consumed_items.append(item)
 time.sleep(random.uniform(0.1, 0.3))
 
 # Create producer and consumer threads
 p = threading.Thread(target=producer, args=(5,))
 c = threading.Thread(target=consumer, args=(5,))
 
 start = time.time()
 p.start()
 c.start()
 p.join()
 c.join()
 
 print(f"\nProducer-Consumer Demo Complete:")
 print(f" Produced: {len(produced_items)}, Consumed: {len(consumed_items)}")

# ============================================
# Main execution
# ============================================
if __name__ == "__main__":
 print("=" * 50)
 print("COORDINATING PROCESSES AND THREADS - Python Demo")
 print("=" * 50 + "\n")
 
 demonstrate_mutex()
 binary_semaphore_demo()
 producer_consumer_demo()
examples.explanation: This Python implementation covers three essential coordination mechanisms: (1) The Counter class with threading.Lock demonstrates mutex for critical section - the 'with' statement ensures lock release even on exceptions. (2) BinarySemaphore class implements Dijkstra's P/V operations using Condition variables - only one thread can hold it at a time (value 0 or 1). (3) BoundedBuffer demonstrates counting semaphores for the producer-consumer problem - empty_slots starts at buffer capacity, full_slots at 0, controlling how many items can be produced/consumed. All three satisfy mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting.

examples.title: C: POSIX Thread Synchronization with Mutex and Semaphores
examples.description: C implementation using POSIX threads (pthread) demonstrating mutex locks and POSIX semaphores for thread coordination
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates three fundamental coordination mechanisms using POSIX threads: (1) Mutex for critical section - SharedCounter struct contains a pthread_mutex_t that protects the counter variable during read-modify-write operations, satisfying mutual exclusion. (2) Binary semaphore using sem_init with value 1 - allows only one thread in the critical section at a time, demonstrating Dijkstra's P(wait)/V(signal) operations. (3) Producer-Consumer with counting semaphores - empty_slots initialized to BUFFER_SIZE controls production, full_slots initialized to 0 controls consumption, and mutex ensures atomic buffer access. The solution correctly handles bounded waiting and prevents race conditions by using sem_wait() and sem_post() atomic operations.

examples.title: Java: Monitor-based Synchronization with synchronized and wait/notify
examples.description: Java implementation demonstrating monitor pattern, intrinsic locks, wait/notify for thread coordination
examples.explanation: This Java implementation demonstrates monitor-based synchronization, which is a high-level language construct: (1) The Counter class with synchronized blocks shows the monitor pattern - Java's 'synchronized' keyword automatically acquires/releases the lock, and only one thread can execute synchronized code on the same object, satisfying mutual exclusion. (2) BinarySemaphore class implements wait()/notify() manually - wait() calls monitor.wait() which releases the lock and blocks, notify() wakes one thread, demonstrating the classic Dijkstra P/V operations. (3) BlockingQueue demonstrates producer-consumer with built-in counting semaphores - put() blocks when full (empty_slots semaphore), take() blocks when empty (full_slots semaphore). (4) CountDownLatch demonstrates barrier synchronization for bounded waiting - all threads must reach the barrier before any can proceed, satisfying the critical section requirement.
